OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation Table

Generative Models via Transportation

The preceding gradient-flow calculus is variational. Modern machine-learning models often use the same transportation language more broadly: one may prescribe an interpolation and regress its velocity, fit a one-step generator to a descent field, or view network depth as a continuous transport of token measures. The examples below separate what is genuinely a Wasserstein gradient flow from what is a transportation dynamics with a useful geometric interpretation.

Guiding Comparison

Flow matching prescribes paths and learns their Eulerian velocity. Diffusion models choose stochastic or noising paths whose reverse probability flow can be sampled. One-step and drifting methods try to store a whole measure evolution inside a single generator update. Transformer depth can also be read as a transport equation, but usually not as a Wasserstein gradient flow of a fixed energy.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Generative Models via Flow Matching¶

Flow matching constructs a generative map by learning the velocity field of an interpolation. The key computational insight is that a constrained continuity-equation problem can be trained by an unconstrained regression.

Generative models aim to build a transportation map TTT between a reference distribution α\alphaα (typically an isotropic Gaussian) and the target data distribution β\betaβ. Since such reference measures are non-atomic, a measurable map with T♯α=βT_\sharp\alpha=\betaT♯​α=β exists on standard Borel spaces, for instance by identifying both probability spaces with the unit interval and using a quantile-type rearrangement. This abstract existence statement is much weaker than having an explicit and numerically stable construction of TTT. Optimal transport is one approach to achieving this, but it is computationally expensive and raises questions about how to estimate it from samples. A different route is to prescribe an interpolation between noise and data, learn its velocity, and obtain TTT by integrating a time-dependent vector field vtv_tvt​. This point of view sits at the meeting point of two literatures, surveyed from a transport perspective in Peyré, 2025. The diffusion branch builds on score matching Hyvärinen, 2005, denoising score matching Vincent, 2011, nonequilibrium noising chains Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015, denoising diffusion probabilistic models Ho et al., 2020, score-based generative modeling Song & Ermon, 2019, and the continuous-time score-SDE/probability-flow formulation Song et al., 2021. The deterministic regression branch was introduced, essentially in parallel, under three closely related names: flow matching Lipman et al., 2023, rectified flow Liu et al., 2023, and stochastic interpolants Albergo et al., 2025. In all three cases, the computational object is a velocity field whose regression loss avoids simulating the learned ODE during training. This vector field vtv_tvt​ is obtained by constructing an interpolation αt\alpha_tαt​ and then finding vtv_tvt​ using the least-squares formula of the dynamic chapter. As we will explain, for a specific class of interpolation (obtained by a parametric push-forward), this vtv_tvt​ can be obtained by avoiding explicitly inverting a Laplacian and instead computing a simple conditional expectation. This conditional expectation can itself be estimated by solving another least-squares problem, but this time unconstrained, making the estimation feasible from finite samples of α\alphaα and β\betaβ.

Stochastic interpolant.¶

We assume that αt\alpha_tαt​ is defined via a “projection” (in a loose sense) of a latent distribution π∈P(Rd′)\pi \in \Pp(\RR^{d'})π∈P(Rd′), using an operator Pt:Rd′→RdP_t : \RR^{d'} \to \RR^dPt​:Rd′→Rd where d′≫dd' \gg dd′≫d, i.e.

∀t∈[0,1],αt:=(Pt)♯π.\forall t \in [0,1], \quad \alpha_t := (P_t)_\sharp \pi.∀t∈[0,1],αt​:=(Pt​)♯​π.
(1)

A common case is d′=2dd'=2dd′=2d. We write (x,y)∈Rd′=Rd×Rd(x,y) \in \RR^{d'} = \RR^d \times \RR^d(x,y)∈Rd′=Rd×Rd and assume P0(x,y)=xP_0(x,y)=xP0​(x,y)=x and P1(x,y)=yP_1(x,y)=yP1​(x,y)=y, so that π\piπ is a probabilistic coupling between α0\alpha_0α0​ and α1\alpha_1α1​, i.e. π\piπ has marginals (α0,α1)(\alpha_0, \alpha_1)(α0​,α1​). For instance, one can use π=α0⊗α1\pi = \alpha_0 \otimes \alpha_1π=α0​⊗α1​, the trivial coupling. An even more special case is the linear interpolation Pt(x,y)=(1−t)x+tyP_t(x,y)=(1-t)x+tyPt​(x,y)=(1−t)x+ty. With this linear path and an arbitrary coupling π\piπ, the regression below is the common core of flow matching and rectified flow: Lipman et al. emphasize conditional probability paths and simulation-free training of continuous normalizing flows, while rectified flow emphasizes straight couplings, reflow, and the possibility of reducing transport costs and discretization error Lipman et al., 2023Liu et al., 2023. More complex constructions are also possible, provided that sampling from π\piπ remains simple; stochastic interpolants allow additional latent variables and stochastic perturbations, thereby unifying deterministic flows, probability-flow ODEs and diffusion SDEs Albergo et al., 2025.

If π=α⊗β\pi = \alpha \otimes \betaπ=α⊗β and α=1n∑iδxi\alpha = \frac{1}{n} \sum_i \delta_{x_i}α=n1​∑i​δxi​​, β=1m∑jδyj\beta = \frac{1}{m} \sum_j \delta_{y_j}β=m1​∑j​δyj​​, then αt\alpha_tαt​ consists of n×mn \times mn×m Dirac masses

αt=1nm∑i,jδPt(xi,yj).\alpha_t = \frac{1}{nm} \sum_{i,j} \delta_{P_t(x_i,y_j)}.αt​=nm1​i,j∑​δPt​(xi​,yj​)​.
(2)

If π=(Id,T)♯α\pi = (\Id, T)_\sharp \alphaπ=(Id,T)♯​α is a Brenier-type coupling, then αt=((1−t)Id+tT)♯α\alpha_t = ((1-t)\Id + tT)_\sharp \alphaαt​=((1−t)Id+tT)♯​α is the so-called McCann OT interpolation.

show_book_figure("generative-flow-matching-interpolants", width=760)

Flow matching interpolants between the same empirical source and target measures. A product-style random pairing produces crossing paths, an OT pairing gives direct displacement rays, and a curved bridge changes the path geometry while keeping the same endpoints. Gray arrows mark representative midpoint velocities ∂tPt\partial_tP_t∂t​Pt​.

Interactive panel. Use the interpolation and noise controls to compare flow-matching paths between source noise and target structure.

Flow matching formula.¶

This interpolation is not directly useful for sampling from β\betaβ, but it can be used to define a flow field vtv_tvt​ so that the continuity equation, in Eulerian form, holds. This flow field is computed by solving an unconstrained least-squares problem, or equivalently, it is a conditional expectation.

Proposition: Flow Matching Vector Field

For each fixed ttt, assume ∂tPt∈L2(π;Rd)\partial_tP_t\in L^2(\pi;\RR^d)∂t​Pt​∈L2(π;Rd). The solution of the flow-matching problem over measurable fields vt:Rd→Rdv_t:\RR^d\to\RR^dvt​:Rd→Rd

min⁡vt∫Rd′∥vt(Pt(u))−[∂tPt](u)∥2  dπ(u).\min_{v_t} \int_{\RR^{d'}} \norm{v_t(P_t(u)) - [\partial_t P_t](u)}^2 \, \d\pi(u).vt​min​∫Rd′​∥vt​(Pt​(u))−[∂t​Pt​](u)∥2dπ(u).
(3)

Equivalently, the minimizer is characterized αt\alpha_tαt​-almost everywhere by the conditional expectation

vt(z)=Eu∼π([∂tPt](u) ∣ z=Pt(u)).v_t(z) = \EE_{u \sim \pi} \big( [\partial_t P_t](u) \, \big| \, z = P_t(u) \big).vt​(z)=Eu∼π​([∂t​Pt​](u)∣∣​z=Pt​(u)).
(4)

Then the pair (αt,vt)(\alpha_t,v_t)(αt​,vt​) satisfies the continuity equation in Eulerian form.

Proof

We first recall the two equivalent ways of writing the interpolated measure. Formally, one may write

αt(z)=∫Rd′δ(z−Pt(u))  dπ(u),\alpha_t(z)=\int_{\RR^{d'}}\delta(z-P_t(u))\,\d\pi(u),αt​(z)=∫Rd′​δ(z−Pt​(u))dπ(u),
(5)

while the rigorous meaning is that, for every smooth test function φ\varphiφ,

∫Rdφ(z)  dαt(z)=∫Rd′φ(Pt(u))  dπ(u).\int_{\RR^d}\varphi(z)\,\d\alpha_t(z) = \int_{\RR^{d'}}\varphi(P_t(u))\,\d\pi(u).∫Rd​φ(z)dαt​(z)=∫Rd′​φ(Pt​(u))dπ(u).
(6)

The minimizer in (3) is the orthogonal projection in L2(π;Rd)L^2(\pi;\RR^d)L2(π;Rd) of the latent velocity ∂tPt(u)\partial_tP_t(u)∂t​Pt​(u) onto the closed subspace of functions that depend on uuu only through Pt(u)P_t(u)Pt​(u). This projection is the conditional expectation (4). Formally, this can be read as

vt(z)=1αt(z)∫Rd′δ(z−Pt(u))[∂tPt](u)  dπ(u),v_t(z)=\frac{1}{\alpha_t(z)} \int_{\RR^{d'}}\delta(z-P_t(u))[\partial_tP_t](u)\,\d\pi(u),vt​(z)=αt​(z)1​∫Rd′​δ(z−Pt​(u))[∂t​Pt​](u)dπ(u),
(7)

and rigorously it means that, for every smooth test vector field mmm,

∫⟨m(z),vt(z)⟩  dαt(z)=∫⟨m(Pt(u)),[∂tPt](u)⟩  dπ(u).\int \dotp{m(z)}{v_t(z)} \, \d\alpha_t(z) = \int \dotp{m(P_t(u))}{[\partial_t P_t](u)} \, \d\pi(u).∫⟨m(z),vt​(z)⟩dαt​(z)=∫⟨m(Pt​(u)),[∂t​Pt​](u)⟩dπ(u).
(8)

We now prove that this field transports the curve (αt)t(\alpha_t)_t(αt​)t​. The weak form of ∂tαt+div⁡(αtvt)=0\partial_t\alpha_t+\operatorname{div}(\alpha_t v_t)=0∂t​αt​+div(αt​vt​)=0 is that, for every smooth scalar test function φ\varphiφ,

 d dt∫φ(z)  dαt(z)−∫⟨vt(z),∇φ(z)⟩  dαt(z)=0.\frac{\d}{\d t}\int\varphi(z)\,\d\alpha_t(z) - \int\dotp{v_t(z)}{\nabla\varphi(z)}\,\d\alpha_t(z) =0.dtd​∫φ(z)dαt​(z)−∫⟨vt​(z),∇φ(z)⟩dαt​(z)=0.
(9)

Using (6) and differentiating under the integral sign gives

 d dt∫φ(z) dαt(z)=∫⟨∇φ(Pt(u)),[∂tPt](u)⟩ dπ(u).\frac{\d}{\d t}\int \varphi(z)\d\alpha_t(z) = \int \dotp{\nabla\varphi(P_t(u))}{[\partial_t P_t](u)}\d\pi(u).dtd​∫φ(z)dαt​(z)=∫⟨∇φ(Pt​(u)),[∂t​Pt​](u)⟩dπ(u).
(10)

On the other hand, applying (8) with m=∇φm=\nabla\varphim=∇φ gives

∫⟨vt(z),∇φ(z)⟩  dαt(z)=∫⟨∇φ(Pt(u)),[∂tPt](u)⟩ dπ(u).\int\dotp{v_t(z)}{\nabla\varphi(z)}\,\d\alpha_t(z) = \int \dotp{\nabla\varphi(P_t(u))}{[\partial_t P_t](u)}\d\pi(u).∫⟨vt​(z),∇φ(z)⟩dαt​(z)=∫⟨∇φ(Pt​(u)),[∂t​Pt​](u)⟩dπ(u).
(11)

Comparing (10) and (11) yields (9), which is the desired continuity equation.

The conditional expectation in (4) has a simple measure-theoretic meaning. Let αt=(Pt)♯π\alpha_t=(P_t)_\sharp\piαt​=(Pt​)♯​π and define the vector-valued measure mtm_tmt​ on Rd\RR^dRd by

∫Rd⟨ψ(z), dmt(z)⟩:=∫Rd′⟨ψ(Pt(u)),[∂tPt](u)⟩ dπ(u)\int_{\RR^d}\dotp{\psi(z)}{\d m_t(z)} \eqdef \int_{\RR^{d'}}\dotp{\psi(P_t(u))}{[\partial_tP_t](u)}\d\pi(u)∫Rd​⟨ψ(z),dmt​(z)⟩:=∫Rd′​⟨ψ(Pt​(u)),[∂t​Pt​](u)⟩dπ(u)
(12)

for every bounded continuous vector field ψ\psiψ. Since αt(A)=0\alpha_t(A)=0αt​(A)=0 implies π(Pt−1(A))=0\pi(P_t^{-1}(A))=0π(Pt−1​(A))=0, one has mt≪αtm_t\ll\alpha_tmt​≪αt​. The Radon--Nikodym decomposition of mtm_tmt​ with respect to αt\alpha_tαt​ is therefore

 dmt(z)=vt(z) dαt(z),vt= dmt dαt.\d m_t(z)=v_t(z)\d\alpha_t(z), \qquad v_t=\frac{\d m_t}{\d\alpha_t}.dmt​(z)=vt​(z)dαt​(z),vt​=dαt​dmt​​.
(13)

In the language of Lebesgue decomposition, the flux measure mtm_tmt​ has only an absolutely continuous part with respect to αt\alpha_tαt​ and no singular part; the conditional expectation is precisely this density. Equivalently, disintegrating π\piπ with respect to the map PtP_tPt​ gives π( du)=πt,z( du)αt( dz)\pi(\d u)=\pi_{t,z}(\d u)\alpha_t(\d z)π(du)=πt,z​(du)αt​(dz), where πt,z\pi_{t,z}πt,z​ is supported on the fiber {u : Pt(u)=z}\{u\,:\,P_t(u)=z\}{u:Pt​(u)=z}, and

vt(z)=∫{Pt(u)=z}[∂tPt](u) dπt,z(u).v_t(z)=\int_{\{P_t(u)=z\}}[\partial_tP_t](u)\d\pi_{t,z}(u).vt​(z)=∫{Pt​(u)=z}​[∂t​Pt​](u)dπt,z​(u).
(14)

Thus the solution of (3) is the conditional expectation of the velocities ∂tPt\partial_t P_t∂t​Pt​: intuitively, vt(z)v_t(z)vt​(z) is the average velocity of all trajectories passing through zzz. Numerically, (x,t)→vt(x)(x,t) \to v_t(x)(x,t)→vt​(x) can be parameterized by a neural network (e.g., a U-Net for vision tasks) and estimated using stochastic gradient descent on the objective in (3). For the exact field vtv_tvt​, integrating the ODE x˙=vt(x)\dot{x}=v_t(x)x˙=vt​(x) defines a transport map TtT_tTt​. If vtv_tvt​ is regular enough, or more generally if the continuity equation has a unique solution for this velocity, then (Tt)♯α0=αt(T_t)_\sharp\alpha_0=\alpha_t(Tt​)♯​α0​=αt​. Thus the same interpolation as (1) is represented by a deterministic flow rather than by the original coupling. The sampling procedure consists in first drawing X0∼αX_0 \sim \alphaX0​∼α, and then integrating the ODE X˙t=vt(Xt)\dot{X}_t = v_t(X_t)X˙t​=vt​(Xt​) starting with Xt=0=X0X_{t=0} = X_0Xt=0​=X0​. In the ideal exact-field limit, the resulting Xt=1X_{t=1}Xt=1​ is distributed according to α1=β\alpha_1 = \betaα1​=β.

Connection with diffusion models.¶

In the special case where Pt(x,y)=(1−t)x+tyP_t(x,y)=(1-t)x+tyPt​(x,y)=(1−t)x+ty is a linear interpolation and π=α⊗β\pi = \alpha \otimes \betaπ=α⊗β, the curve αt\alpha_tαt​ is a convolution of rescaled versions of α0\alpha_0α0​ and α1\alpha_1α1​. The flow-matching problem (3) becomes

min⁡(vt)t∫Rd×Rd∥vt((1−t)x+ty)−(y−x)∥2  dα0(x) dα1(y).\min_{(v_t)_t} \int_{\RR^{d} \times \RR^d} \norm{v_t( (1-t)x+t y ) - (y-x) }^2 \, \d\alpha_0(x) \d\alpha_1(y).(vt​)t​min​∫Rd×Rd​∥vt​((1−t)x+ty)−(y−x)∥2dα0​(x)dα1​(y).
(15)

When one endpoint is an isotropic Gaussian, this construction is closely related to the probability-flow formulation of diffusion models, up to the usual change of time parametrization Song et al., 2021. This is why flow matching can be viewed both as a deterministic alternative to diffusion training and as a common language for diffusion paths, OT-inspired paths, and rectified paths Lipman et al., 2023Liu et al., 2023Albergo et al., 2025. The next two propositions are written in the noising direction, from a data law α\alphaα to a Gaussian; reversing time gives the corresponding sampling flow. They also give an explicit closed form for vtv_tvt​ and show that it is a gradient field. In this setting, vtv_tvt​ is also the solution of the constrained least-squares problem from the dynamic chapter. The regression (3) is computationally simpler because the continuity equation has already been enforced by the chosen interpolant. To prove this, we rely on Tweedie’s formula, which expresses the optimal Gaussian denoiser through the score, i.e. the gradient of the log-density.

Proposition: Tweedie Identity

Let WWW be a random vector in Rd\RR^{d}Rd with density β\betaβ. For σ>0\sigma>0σ>0, observe

Z  =  W+σ ε,where ε∼N(0,Id) is independent of W.Z \;=\; W + \sigma\,\varepsilon, \quad\text{where } \varepsilon \sim \Gaussian(0,I_{d}) \text{ is independent of } W .Z=W+σε,where ε∼N(0,Id​) is independent of W.
(16)

Denote by

βσ  =  β∗N(0,σ2Id)\beta_\sigma \;=\; \beta * \Gaussian\bigl(0,\sigma^{2}I_{d}\bigr)βσ​=β∗N(0,σ2Id​)
(17)

the density of ZZZ. Then

E[ W∣Z=z]  =  z  +  σ2 ∇log⁡βσ(z)for all z∈Rd.\EE\bigl[\,W \mid Z=z\bigr] \;=\; z \;+\;\sigma^{2}\,\nabla \log \beta_\sigma(z) \qquad\text{for all } z \in \RR^{d}.E[W∣Z=z]=z+σ2∇logβσ​(z)for all z∈Rd.
(18)
Proof

Bayes’ rule gives the conditional density pW∣Z(w∣z)=β(w) φσ(z−w)βσ(z)p_{W|Z}(w\mid z) = \dfrac{\beta(w)\,\varphi_\sigma(z-w)}{\beta_\sigma(z)}pW∣Z​(w∣z)=βσ​(z)β(w)φσ​(z−w)​ with φσ\varphi_\sigmaφσ​ the N(0,σ2Id)\Gaussian(0,\sigma^{2}I_{d})N(0,σ2Id​) density. Hence

E[W∣Z=z]=1βσ(z)∫Rdw β(w) φσ(z−w)  dw.\EE[W\mid Z=z] = \frac{1}{\beta_\sigma(z)} \int_{\RR^{d}} w\, \beta(w)\,\varphi_\sigma(z-w)\,\d w .E[W∣Z=z]=βσ​(z)1​∫Rd​wβ(w)φσ​(z−w)dw.
(19)

Differentiating the Gaussian convolution under the integral sign and using ∇zφσ(z−w)=−σ−2(z−w) φσ(z−w)\nabla_z\varphi_\sigma(z-w) = -\sigma^{-2}(z-w)\,\varphi_\sigma(z-w)∇z​φσ​(z−w)=−σ−2(z−w)φσ​(z−w) yields

∇zβσ(z)=∫β(w) ∇zφσ(z−w)  dw=−σ−2(z−E[W∣Z=z]) βσ(z).\nabla_z\beta_\sigma(z) = \int \beta(w)\,\nabla_z\varphi_\sigma(z-w)\,\d w = -\sigma^{-2}\Bigl(z-\EE[W\mid Z=z]\Bigr)\,\beta_\sigma(z).∇z​βσ​(z)=∫β(w)∇z​φσ​(z−w)dw=−σ−2(z−E[W∣Z=z])βσ​(z).
(20)

Rearranging finishes the proof.

Proposition: Gaussian-Endpoint Flow-Matching Field

Let X∼αX\sim\alphaX∼α and Y∼N(0,Id)Y\sim\Gaussian(0,I_{d})Y∼N(0,Id​) be independent. For t∈(0,1)t\in(0,1)t∈(0,1) set

Zt  =  (1−t) X+t Y,αt=Law⁡(Zt).Z_t \;=\; (1-t)\,X + t\,Y, \qquad \alpha_t =\operatorname{Law}(Z_t).Zt​=(1−t)X+tY,αt​=Law(Zt​).
(21)

The regression minimizer v⋆:Rd×(0,1)→Rdv^\star:\RR^d\times(0,1)\to\RR^dv⋆:Rd×(0,1)→Rd of

min⁡v  ∫01 ⁣∬Rd×Rd∣y−x−v((1−t)x+ty,t)∣2  dα(x)  dN(y)  dt\min_{v}\;\int_{0}^{1}\! \iint_{\RR^{d}\times\RR^{d}} \bigl|y-x-v\bigl((1-t)x+t y,t\bigr)\bigr|^{2}\, \d\alpha(x)\,\d\Gaussian(y)\,\d tvmin​∫01​∬Rd×Rd​∣∣​y−x−v((1−t)x+ty,t)∣∣​2dα(x)dN(y)dt
(22)

is

v⋆(x,t)=−11−t x  −  t1−t ∇log⁡αt(x)(x∈Rd,  t∈(0,1)).v^\star(x,t) = -\frac{1}{1-t}\,x \;-\; \frac{t}{1-t}\,\nabla\log\alpha_t(x) \qquad (x\in\RR^{d},\;t\in(0,1)).v⋆(x,t)=−1−t1​x−1−tt​∇logαt​(x)(x∈Rd,t∈(0,1)).
(23)

In particular, for each t∈(0,1)t\in(0,1)t∈(0,1) this field is a gradient field,

v⋆(⋅,t)=−∇(∥⋅∥22(1−t)+t1−tlog⁡αt).v^\star(\cdot,t)=-\nabla \left( \frac{\norm{\cdot}^2}{2(1-t)} +\frac{t}{1-t}\log\alpha_t \right).v⋆(⋅,t)=−∇(2(1−t)∥⋅∥2​+1−tt​logαt​).
(24)
Proof

Fix t∈(0,1)t\in(0,1)t∈(0,1) and write W=(1−t)XW=(1-t)XW=(1−t)X, σ=t\sigma=tσ=t, so that Zt=W+σ YZ_t = W + \sigma\,YZt​=W+σY matches the setting of Tweedie’s identity. Conditional expectations satisfy v⋆(z,t)=E[Y−X∣Zt=z]=1t E[Zt−W∣Zt=z]− 11−t E[W∣Zt=z].v^\star(z,t) = \EE[Y-X\mid Z_t=z] = \frac{1}{t}\,\EE[Z_t-W\mid Z_t=z] -\,\frac{1}{1-t}\,\EE[W\mid Z_t=z].v⋆(z,t)=E[Y−X∣Zt​=z]=t1​E[Zt​−W∣Zt​=z]−1−t1​E[W∣Zt​=z]. Applying Tweedie’s identity to E[W∣Zt=z]\EE[W\mid Z_t=z]E[W∣Zt​=z] and noting E[Y∣Zt=z]=− t ∇log⁡αt(z)\EE[Y\mid Z_t=z] = -\,t\,\nabla\log\alpha_t(z)E[Y∣Zt​=z]=−t∇logαt​(z) gives the claimed formula.

show_book_figure("generative-diffusion-1d-forward-backward", width=760)

One-dimensional diffusion bridge for a Gaussian-mixture data law. The forward path Zt=(1−t)X+tYZ_t=(1-t)X+tYZt​=(1−t)X+tY smooths the red data density toward a blue Gaussian endpoint. Reversing the probability-flow ODE transports a denser set of blue noise samples back toward the data modes, making the splitting of trajectories across mixture components visible.

Interactive panel. Use the noising time and schedule controls to see the one-dimensional forward and reverse diffusion bridge.

The same probability-flow intuition is visible in two dimensions. For a discrete data law, or more generally for a Gaussian mixture, the noising density is a Gaussian mixture whose score can be evaluated explicitly. This makes it possible to draw backward trajectories without training a neural network. In the plots below, the Gaussian endpoint has covariance σ2Id\sigma^2\Idσ2Id to keep the geometry visible at the scale of the three atoms. For a scalar noising schedule Zt=atX+btYZ_t=a_tX+b_tYZt​=at​X+bt​Y, the intermediate law has component centers atcja_t c_jat​cj​ and covariance (btσ)2Id(b_t\sigma)^2\Id(bt​σ)2Id. For the linear bridge, pt(z)=∑jwjN((1−t)cj,(tσ)2Id)p_t(z)=\sum_j w_j\Gaussian((1-t)c_j,(t\sigma)^2\Id)pt​(z)=∑j​wj​N((1−t)cj​,(tσ)2Id), with st=∇log⁡pts_t=\nabla\log p_tst​=∇logpt​, and the scaled Gaussian-endpoint field gives vt(z)=−(z+tσ2st(z))/(1−t)v_t(z)=-(z+t\sigma^2s_t(z))/(1-t)vt​(z)=−(z+tσ2st​(z))/(1−t).

show_book_figure("generative-diffusion-2d-forward-backward", width=760)

Two-dimensional noising paths from three Dirac masses to a single Gaussian. The linear interpolation Zt=(1−t)X+tYZ_t=(1-t)X+tYZt​=(1−t)X+tY moves component centers linearly toward the origin and grows covariance like (tσ)2Id(t\sigma)^2\Id(tσ)2Id. The variance-preserving Ornstein--Uhlenbeck bridge has the same endpoints but a different speed of contraction and noising.

Interactive panel. Use the schedule and time controls to watch two-dimensional samples blur forward and concentrate backward.

When is the induced map optimal?¶

Integrating the learned velocity gives a deterministic map from α0\alpha_0α0​ to α1\alpha_1α1​, but this map is not automatically the Brenier optimal map. It is optimal only in special cases where the accumulated flow remains the gradient of a convex potential. The Gaussian product-coupling case already shows the precise obstruction: the interpolated covariances are simple, the velocity is affine, but the terminal map can contain a hidden rotational part. This phenomenon, and its extensions to rectified flows and mixtures, is analyzed in depth in Hertrich et al., 2025.

Proposition: Gaussian Flow Matching and Optimality

Let Σ0,Σ1≻0\Sigma_0,\Sigma_1\succ0Σ0​,Σ1​≻0 and let X0∼N(0,Σ0)X_0\sim\Gaussian(0,\Sigma_0)X0​∼N(0,Σ0​) and X1∼N(0,Σ1)X_1\sim\Gaussian(0,\Sigma_1)X1​∼N(0,Σ1​) be independent. Consider the linear flow-matching interpolation

Zt=(1−t)X0+tX1,αt=Law⁡(Zt)=N(0,Σt),Z_t=(1-t)X_0+tX_1, \qquad \alpha_t=\operatorname{Law}(Z_t)=\Gaussian(0,\Sigma_t),Zt​=(1−t)X0​+tX1​,αt​=Law(Zt​)=N(0,Σt​),
(25)

where

Σt=(1−t)2Σ0+t2Σ1.\Sigma_t=(1-t)^2\Sigma_0+t^2\Sigma_1.Σt​=(1−t)2Σ0​+t2Σ1​.
(26)

Then the exact flow-matching velocity is affine, vt(z)=Atzv_t(z)=A_tzvt​(z)=At​z, with

At=(tΣ1−(1−t)Σ0)Σt−1.A_t=\bigl(t\Sigma_1-(1-t)\Sigma_0\bigr)\Sigma_t^{-1}.At​=(tΣ1​−(1−t)Σ0​)Σt−1​.
(27)

The induced flow map TtFMT_t^{\rm FM}TtFM​ from α0\alpha_0α0​ to αt\alpha_tαt​ is

TtFM=Σ01/2((1−t)2Id+t2Σ0−1/2Σ1Σ0−1/2)1/2Σ0−1/2.T_t^{\rm FM} = \Sigma_0^{1/2} \Bigl((1-t)^2\Id+t^2\Sigma_0^{-1/2}\Sigma_1\Sigma_0^{-1/2}\Bigr)^{1/2} \Sigma_0^{-1/2}.TtFM​=Σ01/2​((1−t)2Id+t2Σ0−1/2​Σ1​Σ0−1/2​)1/2Σ0−1/2​.
(28)

In particular,

T1FM=Σ01/2(Σ0−1/2Σ1Σ0−1/2)1/2Σ0−1/2.T_1^{\rm FM} = \Sigma_0^{1/2} \bigl(\Sigma_0^{-1/2}\Sigma_1\Sigma_0^{-1/2}\bigr)^{1/2} \Sigma_0^{-1/2}.T1FM​=Σ01/2​(Σ0−1/2​Σ1​Σ0−1/2​)1/2Σ0−1/2​.
(29)

This terminal map coincides with the quadratic optimal transport map

TOT=Σ0−1/2(Σ01/2Σ1Σ01/2)1/2Σ0−1/2T^{\rm OT} = \Sigma_0^{-1/2} \bigl(\Sigma_0^{1/2}\Sigma_1\Sigma_0^{1/2}\bigr)^{1/2} \Sigma_0^{-1/2}TOT=Σ0−1/2​(Σ01/2​Σ1​Σ01/2​)1/2Σ0−1/2​
(30)

if and only if Σ0Σ1=Σ1Σ0\Sigma_0\Sigma_1=\Sigma_1\Sigma_0Σ0​Σ1​=Σ1​Σ0​.

Proof

The conditional-expectation formula gives

vt(z)=E[X1−X0∣Zt=z].v_t(z)=\EE[X_1-X_0\mid Z_t=z].vt​(z)=E[X1​−X0​∣Zt​=z].
(31)

Since all variables are jointly Gaussian, this conditional expectation is linear and

vt(z)=Cov⁡(X1−X0,Zt)Cov⁡(Zt)−1z=(tΣ1−(1−t)Σ0)Σt−1z,v_t(z) = \operatorname{Cov}(X_1-X_0,Z_t)\operatorname{Cov}(Z_t)^{-1}z = \bigl(t\Sigma_1-(1-t)\Sigma_0\bigr)\Sigma_t^{-1}z,vt​(z)=Cov(X1​−X0​,Zt​)Cov(Zt​)−1z=(tΣ1​−(1−t)Σ0​)Σt−1​z,
(32)

which proves (27). To solve the characteristic equation, whiten the source by setting

C=Σ0−1/2Σ1Σ0−1/2,Z~t=Σ0−1/2Zt.C=\Sigma_0^{-1/2}\Sigma_1\Sigma_0^{-1/2}, \qquad \widetilde Z_t=\Sigma_0^{-1/2}Z_t.C=Σ0−1/2​Σ1​Σ0−1/2​,Zt​=Σ0−1/2​Zt​.
(33)

In these coordinates the source covariance is Id\IdId and

Σ~t=(1−t)2Id+t2C.\widetilde\Sigma_t=(1-t)^2\Id+t^2C.Σt​=(1−t)2Id+t2C.
(34)

Because Id\IdId and CCC commute, the affine flow map in whitened coordinates is simply T~t=Σ~t1/2\widetilde T_t=\widetilde\Sigma_t^{1/2}Tt​=Σt1/2​. Indeed,

 d dtΣ~t1/2=(tC−(1−t)Id)Σ~t−1/2,\frac{\d}{\d t}\widetilde\Sigma_t^{1/2} = \bigl(tC-(1-t)\Id\bigr)\widetilde\Sigma_t^{-1/2},dtd​Σt1/2​=(tC−(1−t)Id)Σt−1/2​,
(35)

which is exactly the equation T~˙t=A~tT~t\dot{\widetilde T}_t=\widetilde A_t\widetilde T_tT˙t​=At​Tt​ with T~0=Id\widetilde T_0=\IdT0​=Id. Returning to the original coordinates gives (28), and t=1t=1t=1 gives (29).

Both T1FMT_1^{\rm FM}T1FM​ and TOTT^{\rm OT}TOT push N(0,Σ0)\Gaussian(0,\Sigma_0)N(0,Σ0​) to N(0,Σ1)\Gaussian(0,\Sigma_1)N(0,Σ1​). The Brenier map between nondegenerate Gaussians is the unique symmetric positive definite linear map with this property. Hence T1FM=TOTT_1^{\rm FM}=T^{\rm OT}T1FM​=TOT if and only if T1FMT_1^{\rm FM}T1FM​ is symmetric positive definite. The map T1FMT_1^{\rm FM}T1FM​ is similar to C1/2C^{1/2}C1/2, so if it is symmetric then it is automatically positive definite. It remains to characterize symmetry. Since C1/2C^{1/2}C1/2 is symmetric positive definite,

(T1FM)⊤=Σ0−1/2C1/2Σ01/2.(T_1^{\rm FM})^\top = \Sigma_0^{-1/2}C^{1/2}\Sigma_0^{1/2}.(T1FM​)⊤=Σ0−1/2​C1/2Σ01/2​.
(36)

Thus symmetry of T1FMT_1^{\rm FM}T1FM​ is equivalent to Σ0C1/2=C1/2Σ0\Sigma_0 C^{1/2}=C^{1/2}\Sigma_0Σ0​C1/2=C1/2Σ0​, hence to Σ0C=CΣ0\Sigma_0 C=C\Sigma_0Σ0​C=CΣ0​ by functional calculus. Multiplying this identity on the left and right by Σ01/2\Sigma_0^{1/2}Σ01/2​ gives Σ0Σ1=Σ1Σ0\Sigma_0\Sigma_1=\Sigma_1\Sigma_0Σ0​Σ1​=Σ1​Σ0​. Conversely, if Σ0\Sigma_0Σ0​ and Σ1\Sigma_1Σ1​ commute, they are orthogonally co-diagonalizable, and both (29) and (30) reduce in that basis to the diagonal map with entries λ1,k/λ0,k\sqrt{\lambda_{1,k}/\lambda_{0,k}}λ1,k​/λ0,k​​. This proves the equivalence.

The Gaussian optimality proposition explains why the statement “flow matching gives an optimal map” is fragile. The same terminal map (29) is obtained for any scalar schedule Zt=atX0+btX1Z_t=a_tX_0+b_tX_1Zt​=at​X0​+bt​X1​ with the same endpoints, because after whitening the covariance path remains at2Id+bt2Ca_t^2\Id+b_t^2Cat2​Id+bt2​C. Thus changing the speed of a scalar Gaussian bridge, for instance by using an OU schedule, cannot repair the non-optimality created by non-commuting covariances. Commuting covariances reduce the terminal map to independent one-dimensional scalings, whereas non-commuting covariances create a non-symmetric affine map, hence a transport with a rotational or shearing component. More generally, mixture-like paths can create the same obstruction even when every instantaneous velocity looks natural. This distinction is closely related to counterexamples showing that flow maps associated with Fokker--Planck or diffusion-type evolutions do not in general provide optimal transport maps Lavenant & Santambrogio, 2022. In particular, starting from an isotropic Gaussian does not by itself guarantee optimality once the target distribution is non-Gaussian; additional structural assumptions on the path or on the coupling are needed.

Variations on the interpolant.¶

The geometry of the generated trajectories depends on the chosen interpolant, not only on the two endpoint laws. There is first a harmless ambiguity: a monotone reparametrization Zt=(1−λ(t))X+λ(t)YZ_t=(1-\lambda(t))X+\lambda(t)YZt​=(1−λ(t))X+λ(t)Y of the linear bridge only changes the speed of the flow,

vt(z)=λ′(t) vλ(t)lin(z),vrlin(z)=E[Y−X∣(1−r)X+rY=z].v_t(z)=\lambda'(t)\,v^{\rm lin}_{\lambda(t)}(z), \qquad v^{\rm lin}_{r}(z)=\EE[Y-X\mid (1-r)X+rY=z].vt​(z)=λ′(t)vλ(t)lin​(z),vrlin​(z)=E[Y−X∣(1−r)X+rY=z].
(37)

It therefore leaves the spatial integral curves unchanged. Diffusion models use a genuinely different family of noising paths. If

Zt=atX+btY,Y∼N(0,σ2Id),Z_t=a_tX+b_tY,\qquad Y\sim\Gaussian(0,\sigma^2\Id),Zt​=at​X+bt​Y,Y∼N(0,σ2Id),
(38)

then both the mixture centers and the component variances are changed. Writing ptp_tpt​ for the density of ZtZ_tZt​ and st=∇log⁡pts_t=\nabla\log p_tst​=∇logpt​, Tweedie’s formula gives, away from times where at=0a_t=0at​=0,

vt(z)=at′ E[X∣Zt=z]+bt′ E[Y∣Zt=z]=at′atz+(at′bt2at−bt′bt)σ2st(z).v_t(z)=a'_t\,\EE[X\mid Z_t=z]+b'_t\,\EE[Y\mid Z_t=z] =\frac{a'_t}{a_t}z+ \left(\frac{a'_tb_t^2}{a_t}-b'_tb_t\right)\sigma^2s_t(z).vt​(z)=at′​E[X∣Zt​=z]+bt′​E[Y∣Zt​=z]=at​at′​​z+(at​at′​bt2​​−bt′​bt​)σ2st​(z).
(39)

For the linear bridge, at=1−ta_t=1-tat​=1−t and bt=tb_t=tbt​=t, this recovers the formula above. For the variance-preserving Ornstein--Uhlenbeck noising used in diffusion models,

aτ=e−τ,bτ=1−e−2τ,a_\tau=e^{-\tau},\qquad b_\tau=\sqrt{1-e^{-2\tau}},aτ​=e−τ,bτ​=1−e−2τ​,
(40)

one obtains the forward probability-flow velocity vτ(z)=−z−σ2∇log⁡pτ(z)v_\tau(z)=-z-\sigma^2\nabla\log p_\tau(z)vτ​(z)=−z−σ2∇logpτ​(z). Sampling follows the reverse field z+σ2∇log⁡pτ(z)z+\sigma^2\nabla\log p_\tau(z)z+σ2∇logpτ​(z) as τ\tauτ decreases. This is the noising law used in the diffusion trajectory panel below; the trajectories are more curved than for the linear bridge because the centers and variances evolve according to the OU/Fokker--Planck scaling rather than by affine interpolation. Numerically, the integration is stopped at a small positive time before the Dirac endpoint, where the score becomes singular.

The finite-time coefficients at=cos⁡(πt/2)a_t=\cos(\pi t/2)at​=cos(πt/2) and bt=sin⁡(πt/2)b_t=\sin(\pi t/2)bt​=sin(πt/2) are not a new spatial interpolant: they are exactly the OU coefficients after the time change τ=−log⁡cos⁡(πt/2)\tau=-\log\cos(\pi t/2)τ=−logcos(πt/2). The schedule comparison below therefore places the OU bridge next to a genuinely different scalar bridge,

at=(1−t)(1−2t),bt=t,a_t=(1-t)(1-2t), \qquad b_t=t,at​=(1−t)(1−2t),bt​=t,
(41)

whose data coefficient changes sign before vanishing. This overshooting bridge is mainly a diagnostic example: it keeps the same endpoints, but its intermediate mixture reflects through the origin and produces visibly different reverse trajectories.

show_book_figure("generative-diffusion-versus-ot-2d-paths", width=760)

Diffusion-style sampling trajectories compared with OT rays in the three-Dirac setting. Red particles are sampled from the centered Gaussian endpoint and transported toward the three blue atoms. The diffusion panel integrates a reverse probability-flow field based on a Gaussian-mixture score, while the OT panel uses straight displacement rays selected by a quadratic matching.

Interactive panel. Use the trajectory and schedule controls to compare curved diffusion sampling paths with straight optimal-transport rays.

show_book_figure("generative-diffusion-schedule-comparison", width=760)

Effect of the interpolant on the exact reverse flow for the same three-Dirac target and the same Gaussian endpoint. The linear bridge at=1−ta_t=1-tat​=1−t, bt=tb_t=tbt​=t produces almost radial curves. The variance-preserving OU bridge aτ=e−τa_\tau=e^{-\tau}aτ​=e−τ, bτ=1−e−2τb_\tau=\sqrt{1-e^{-2\tau}}bτ​=1−e−2τ​ changes the relative speed of contraction and noising. The overshooting bridge at=(1−t)(1−2t)a_t=(1-t)(1-2t)at​=(1−t)(1−2t), bt=tb_t=tbt​=t is not a time reparameterization of either one and produces a more pronounced bending of the reverse trajectories.

Interactive panel. Use the schedule controls to compare how different noising laws allocate motion over time.

One-Step Generative Models¶

One-step generative models try to keep the geometric training principle of flows while removing the expensive multi-step integration at sampling time. The idea is to evolve the model distribution during training, but to store the final evolution in a single generator evaluation.

Training a one-step flow.¶

Let ζ\zetaζ be a simple latent distribution and let αθ=(Gθ)♯ζ\alpha_\theta=(G_\theta)_\sharp\zetaαθ​=(Gθ​)♯​ζ be the model distribution. Assume that the target data distribution is β\betaβ. A Wasserstein-flow construction chooses a discrepancy

Eβ(α),\mathcal E_\beta(\alpha),Eβ​(α),
(42)

for instance a smoothed KL⁡(α∣β)\KL(\alpha|\beta)KL(α∣β), an MMD/IPM loss, or the debiased Sinkhorn divergence Lˉcϵ(α,β)\bar\MK_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)Lˉcϵ​(α,β) introduced in the Sinkhorn divergence section. The associated formal descent is

∂tμt+div⁡(μtwt)=0,wt(x)=−∇δαEβ(μt)(x).\partial_t\mu_t+\operatorname{div}(\mu_t w_t)=0, \qquad w_t(x)=-\nabla\delta_\alpha \mathcal E_\beta(\mu_t)(x).∂t​μt​+div(μt​wt​)=0,wt​(x)=−∇δα​Eβ​(μt​)(x).
(43)

Instead of integrating (43) at inference time, one fits a parametric residual field UηU_\etaUη​ along the current model distribution:

min⁡η∫01 ⁣∫∥Uη(t,x)−wt(x)∥2  dμt(x)  dt.\min_\eta \int_0^1\!\int \norm{U_\eta(t,x)-w_t(x)}^2 \,\d\mu_t(x)\,\d t.ηmin​∫01​∫∥Uη​(t,x)−wt​(x)∥2dμt​(x)dt.
(44)

In a particle or generator implementation, the learned residual is then used to update the current generator by

αθ+=(Id+τUη)♯αθ,or equivalentlyGθ+(z)=Gθ(z)+τUη(Gθ(z)).\alpha_{\theta}^{+} = (\Id+\tau U_\eta)_\sharp \alpha_\theta, \qquad\text{or equivalently}\qquad G_\theta^{+}(z)=G_\theta(z)+\tau U_\eta(G_\theta(z)).αθ+​=(Id+τUη​)♯​αθ​,or equivalentlyGθ+​(z)=Gθ​(z)+τUη​(Gθ​(z)).
(45)

After many training updates, the accumulated generator is evaluated once at test time. This is the organizing principle behind recent one-step methods based on Wasserstein gradient flows: W-Flow uses such a construction with the Sinkhorn divergence as a tractable global discrepancy Han et al., 2026, while drifting methods evolve the generated distribution during training through a fitted vector field and also admit one-step inference Deng et al., 2026. The gradient-flow interpretation of drifting models, and its relation to KL, MMD, sliced-Wasserstein and Sinkhorn-type discrepancies, is analyzed in Gretton et al., 2026He et al., 2026. These ideas are also connected to the Sinkhorn-type normalization dynamics used to model attention in Sinkformers Sander et al., 2022.

Self-corrected drifting fields.¶

Drifting methods need not start from an exact Wasserstein gradient. They often prescribe an attraction-minus-repulsion field and then regress this field in L2(μt)L^2(\mu_t)L2(μt​). A simple continuous version uses a positive kernel Kϵ(x,y)K_\epsilon(x,y)Kϵ​(x,y) and defines, for any measure ν\nuν,

Bϵ[ν](x):=∫(y−x)Kϵ(x,y)  dν(y)∫Kϵ(x,y)  dν(y).B_\epsilon[\nu](x) \eqdef \frac{\int (y-x)K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\nu(y)} {\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\nu(y)}.Bϵ​[ν](x):=∫Kϵ​(x,y)dν(y)∫(y−x)Kϵ​(x,y)dν(y)​.
(46)

For the Gaussian kernel Kϵ(x,y)=exp⁡(−∥x−y∥2/(2ϵ))K_\epsilon(x,y)=\exp(-\norm{x-y}^2/(2\epsilon))Kϵ​(x,y)=exp(−∥x−y∥2/(2ϵ)), this normalized field is a score of a smoothed density:

Bϵ[ν](x)=ϵ∇log⁡ ⁣(∫Kϵ(x,y)  dν(y)).B_\epsilon[\nu](x) = \epsilon\nabla\log\!\left(\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\nu(y)\right).Bϵ​[ν](x)=ϵ∇log(∫Kϵ​(x,y)dν(y)).
(47)

The drifting velocity is then

ut(x)=Bϵ[β](x)−Bϵ[μt](x)=ϵ∇log⁡∫Kϵ(x,y)  dβ(y)∫Kϵ(x,y)  dμt(y).u_t(x)=B_\epsilon[\beta](x)-B_\epsilon[\mu_t](x) = \epsilon\nabla\log \frac{\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\beta(y)} {\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\mu_t(y)}.ut​(x)=Bϵ​[β](x)−Bϵ​[μt​](x)=ϵ∇log∫Kϵ​(x,y)dμt​(y)∫Kϵ​(x,y)dβ(y)​.
(48)

The first term pulls samples toward data, while the second term corrects self-attraction and prevents all particles from collapsing onto the same high-density region. Sinkhorn drifting replaces these one-sided kernel normalizations by two-sided entropic OT couplings, so that the cross and self terms are normalized by Sinkhorn scaling rather than by a single denominator He et al., 2026.

show_book_figure("generative-drifting-model-trajectories", width=760)

Drifting trajectories for a small particle generator. The raw kernel drift has weak long-range attraction and can leave particles away from the data modes. The self-corrected field uses the difference Bϵ[β]−Bϵ[μt]B_\epsilon[\beta]-B_\epsilon[\mu_t]Bϵ​[β]−Bϵ​[μt​], so a longer integration brings particles to the blue modes while repelling them from their own current concentration.

Interactive panel. Use the drift and time controls to inspect a learned-looking velocity field and its induced particle trajectories.

Proposition: Drifting as a Time-Dependent Wasserstein Gradient

Let μt\mu_tμt​ be a smooth curve of positive densities and let ut=∇ϕtu_t=\nabla\phi_tut​=∇ϕt​ be a smooth time-dependent gradient field. Define the semi-relaxed functional

Rt(α∣μt):=−∫ϕt(x)  dα(x)+∫ϕt(x)  dμt(x).\mathcal R_t(\alpha|\mu_t) \eqdef -\int \phi_t(x)\,\d\alpha(x) +\int \phi_t(x)\,\d\mu_t(x).Rt​(α∣μt​):=−∫ϕt​(x)dα(x)+∫ϕt​(x)dμt​(x).
(49)

Here μt\mu_tμt​ and ϕt\phi_tϕt​ are frozen when taking the first variation with respect to the first argument α\alphaα. Then the continuity equation

∂tμt+div⁡(μtut)=0\partial_t\mu_t+\operatorname{div}(\mu_t u_t)=0∂t​μt​+div(μt​ut​)=0
(50)

is the formal Wasserstein gradient descent of the time-dependent functional α↦Rt(α∣μt)\alpha\mapsto\mathcal R_t(\alpha|\mu_t)α↦Rt​(α∣μt​).

Proof

Since μt\mu_tμt​ and ϕt\phi_tϕt​ are fixed in the variation with respect to α\alphaα, the first variation is

δαRt(α∣μt)(x)=−ϕt(x).\delta_\alpha \mathcal R_t(\alpha|\mu_t)(x)=-\phi_t(x).δα​Rt​(α∣μt​)(x)=−ϕt​(x).
(51)

By the Wasserstein-gradient formula,

∇ ⁣WRt(α∣μt)=∇δαRt(α∣μt)=−∇ϕt=−ut.\Wgrad \mathcal R_t(\alpha|\mu_t) = \nabla\delta_\alpha \mathcal R_t(\alpha|\mu_t) = -\nabla\phi_t = -u_t.∇W​Rt​(α∣μt​)=∇δα​Rt​(α∣μt​)=−∇ϕt​=−ut​.
(52)

The Wasserstein gradient-descent velocity is the negative of this gradient, namely utu_tut​. Substituting this velocity in the continuity equation gives the claimed flow.

Example

For the Gaussian-kernel drift (48), set

ϕt(x)=ϵlog⁡∫Kϵ(x,y)  dβ(y)∫Kϵ(x,y)  dμt(y).\phi_t(x)= \epsilon\log \frac{\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\beta(y)} {\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\mu_t(y)}.ϕt​(x)=ϵlog∫Kϵ​(x,y)dμt​(y)∫Kϵ​(x,y)dβ(y)​.
(53)

Then ut=∇ϕtu_t=\nabla\phi_tut​=∇ϕt​, so the semi-relaxed-gradient proposition shows that kernel drifting is the Wasserstein gradient descent of

Rtdrift(α∣μt)=ϵ∫log⁡∫Kϵ(x,y)  dμt(y)∫Kϵ(x,y)  dβ(y)  dα(x)+constant.\mathcal R_t^{\mathrm{drift}}(\alpha|\mu_t) = \epsilon \int \log \frac{\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\mu_t(y)} {\int K_\epsilon(x,y)\,\d\beta(y)} \,\d\alpha(x) +\mathrm{constant}.Rtdrift​(α∣μt​)=ϵ∫log∫Kϵ​(x,y)dβ(y)∫Kϵ​(x,y)dμt​(y)​dα(x)+constant.
(54)

It is “semi-relaxed” because the current model μt\mu_tμt​ is used to build the potential, but it is not varied inside the denominator when computing the first variation in α\alphaα.

Remark

A general regressed field btb_tbt​ is not necessarily a Wasserstein gradient, since Wasserstein tangent vectors are represented by gradient fields modulo L2(μt)L^2(\mu_t)L2(μt​)-null directions. The gradient component is obtained by the weighted projection

∇ϕt=arg min⁡∇ϕ∫∥∇ϕ(x)−bt(x)∥2  dμt(x).\nabla\phi_t = \argmin_{\nabla\phi} \int \norm{\nabla\phi(x)-b_t(x)}^2\,\d\mu_t(x).∇ϕt​=∇ϕargmin​∫∥∇ϕ(x)−bt​(x)∥2dμt​(x).
(55)

One may first normalize btb_tbt​ pointwise, for instance by bt/(∥bt∥+η)b_t/(\norm{b_t}+\eta)bt​/(∥bt​∥+η), or globally by ∥bt∥L2(μt)\norm{b_t}_{L^2(\mu_t)}∥bt​∥L2(μt​)​, before this projection. The semi-relaxed-gradient proposition then applies to the projected field. Non-gradient components can still be useful in a parametric model, but they are not descent directions of a scalar functional for the W2\Wass_2W2​ Riemannian metric.

Evolution in Depth of Transformers¶

Deep residual architectures can be read as time discretizations of ODEs or PDEs. For transformers, the transported objects are token measures and the velocity is induced by attention.

Transformers were introduced as sequence-to-sequence architectures driven by self-attention Vaswani et al., 2017 and have since become a central architecture for language and vision models Brown et al., 2020Dosovitskiy et al., 2021. Their distinctive feature is that each token is updated by a data-dependent average of all other tokens. This makes an attention layer permutation-equivariant before positional encoding, context dependent after conditioning on the input sequence, and naturally compatible with a measure viewpoint in which a prompt is regarded as an empirical distribution of tokens.

The mathematical limit used below concerns depth rather than model scale: one lets the number of residual attention layers grow while each layer makes a small update, as in continuous-depth neural networks Chen et al., 2018. For attention, the resulting velocity is nonlinear in the current token law because it is normalized by the whole context. This measure-theoretic view appears in the analysis of attention as a Lipschitz or interacting-particle operator Vuckovic et al., 2020Geshkovski et al., 2023, in the Sinkhorn-normalized dynamics of Sinkformers Sander et al., 2022, and in recent well-posedness and mean-field-limit results for several attention mechanisms Castin et al., 2025. It also separates the infinite-depth limit studied here from the token-limit question, where one controls how a finite empirical context approximates its limiting attention operator Bohbot et al., 2025.

We now consider very deep transformers, focusing on a single-head attention mechanism for simplicity while ignoring MLP layers, layer normalization, causality, and masking. This stripped-down framework is best suited to modeling encoders and vision transformers; the references above indicate which parts of this simplified picture extend to richer attention mechanisms.

Attention as a context-dependent velocity.¶

After tokenization, embedding, and positional encoding, each input (from a set of tokens) is represented as a point cloud (xi)i=1n(x_i)_{i=1}^n(xi​)i=1n​ of nnn points in the space of vectorized tokens. An attention layer with skip connection and rescaling by 1/T1/T1/T (where TTT is the depth) defines a transformation of the tokens:

xi↦xi+1T∑je⟨Qxi,Kxj⟩Vxj∑ℓe⟨Qxi,Kxℓ⟩,x_i \mapsto x_i + \frac{1}{T} \sum_j \frac{e^{\langle Q x_i, K x_j \rangle} V x_j}{\sum_{\ell} e^{\langle Q x_i, K x_\ell \rangle}},xi​↦xi​+T1​j∑​∑ℓ​e⟨Qxi​,Kxℓ​⟩e⟨Qxi​,Kxj​⟩Vxj​​,
(56)

where θ=(K,Q,V)\theta = (K, Q, V)θ=(K,Q,V) are the parameters of the attention layer, represented by three matrices.

Token measure evolution.¶

To handle an arbitrary number of tokens, we define α=1n∑iδxi\alpha = \frac{1}{n} \sum_i \delta_{x_i}α=n1​∑i​δxi​​ as the empirical measure of tokens and rewrite the transformer mapping as:

xi↦xi+1TΓθ[α](xi),x_i \mapsto x_i + \frac{1}{T} \Gamma_\theta[\alpha](x_i),xi​↦xi​+T1​Γθ​[α](xi​),
(57)

where

Γθ[α](x):=∫e⟨Qx,Ky⟩Vy  dα(y)∫e⟨Qx,Kz⟩  dα(z).\Gamma_\theta[\alpha](x) := \frac{\int e^{\langle Q x, K y \rangle} V y \, \d \alpha(y)} {\int e^{\langle Q x, K z \rangle} \, \d \alpha(z)}.Γθ​[α](x):=∫e⟨Qx,Kz⟩dα(z)∫e⟨Qx,Ky⟩Vydα(y)​.
(58)

At the level of the token distribution, the layer pushes α\alphaα forward by the “in-context” mapping Γθt[α]\Gamma_{\theta_t}[\alpha]Γθt​​[α], which depends on the context α\alphaα, the tokens, and the depth-dependent parameters θt\theta_tθt​. Denoting t∈[0,1]t \in [0, 1]t∈[0,1] as the depth and τ=1/T\tau = 1/Tτ=1/T as the step size, this gives:

αt+τ=(Id+τΓθt[αt])♯αt.\alpha_{t+\tau} = (\Id + \tau \Gamma_{\theta_t}[\alpha_t])_\sharp \alpha_t.αt+τ​=(Id+τΓθt​​[αt​])♯​αt​.
(59)

As τ→0\tau \to 0τ→0, this converges formally to the conservation equation

∂tαt+div⁡(αtΓθt[αt])=0.\partial_t \alpha_t + \operatorname{div}(\alpha_t \Gamma_{\theta_t}[\alpha_t]) = 0.∂t​αt​+div(αt​Γθt​​[αt​])=0.
(60)

Gradient structure and limitations.¶

When the token space has dimension ddd and the query/key space has dimension rrr, take Q,K∈Rr×dQ,K\in\RR^{r\times d}Q,K∈Rr×d and V∈Rd×dV\in\RR^{d\times d}V∈Rd×d. If V=Q⊤KV=Q^\top KV=Q⊤K, the field Γθ[α]\Gamma_\theta[\alpha]Γθ​[α] is a gradient vector field in the token variable. Indeed, define the log-partition potential

Φα(x)=∫exp⁡(⟨Qx,Ky⟩) dα(y),Uα(x)=log⁡Φα(x).\Phi_\alpha(x) = \int \exp(\dotp{Qx}{Ky})\d\alpha(y), \qquad U_\alpha(x)=\log\Phi_\alpha(x).Φα​(x)=∫exp(⟨Qx,Ky⟩)dα(y),Uα​(x)=logΦα​(x).
(61)

Then

∇xUα(x)=∫Q⊤Ky exp⁡(⟨Qx,Ky⟩) dα(y)∫exp⁡(⟨Qx,Kz⟩) dα(z)=Γθ[α](x).\nabla_x U_\alpha(x) = \frac{\int Q^\top K y\,\exp(\dotp{Qx}{Ky})\d\alpha(y)} {\int \exp(\dotp{Qx}{Kz})\d\alpha(z)} = \Gamma_\theta[\alpha](x).∇x​Uα​(x)=∫exp(⟨Qx,Kz⟩)dα(z)∫Q⊤Kyexp(⟨Qx,Ky⟩)dα(y)​=Γθ​[α](x).
(62)

This is an instantaneous gradient in xxx. It is not, however, the gradient of the first variation of a fixed functional of α\alphaα, because the potential UαU_\alphaUα​ itself depends on the current measure through the same attention normalization. Thus the PDE is generally a transportation dynamics, not a Wasserstein gradient flow. Special variants recover additional structure: Sinkhorn attention can be interpreted through doubly stochastic normalization and Wasserstein-type gradient flows Sander et al., 2022Castin et al., 2025, while layer normalization leads naturally to dynamics on the sphere and to modified metrics. The key open difficulty for the present viewpoint is training: after the architecture has been rewritten as a controlled transport equation, learning corresponds to optimizing the time-dependent parameters (θt)t(\theta_t)_t(θt​)t​ rather than merely analyzing the forward PDE for fixed parameters.

Flows over the Gaussian Manifold¶

Gaussian measures provide a useful testing ground for the preceding dynamics. They are not invariant under a general Wasserstein gradient flow: a nonlinear velocity usually creates non-Gaussian densities immediately. The useful substitute is to either identify affine velocities, which exactly preserve Gaussianity, or to project the dynamics onto the Gaussian manifold. In both cases the measure PDE reduces to matrix ODEs for the mean and covariance. This viewpoint is emphasized in the survey Peyré, 2025 and is useful for comparing diffusion paths, Wasserstein gradient flows, drifting fields and transformer-type dynamics.

For constrained gradient flows on this family, the covariance equation is the finite-dimensional Bures--Wasserstein gradient flow on positive definite matrices. Thus Gaussian closure is not just a computational shortcut: it is the restriction of Wasserstein geometry to the Gaussian submanifold, where affine gradient fields encode tangent vectors.

show_book_figure("gradflow-gaussian-closure", width=760)

Gaussian closures of transport dynamics between two overlapping anisotropic Gaussians. The left panel is the exact W2\Wass_2W2​ Gaussian geodesic. The middle panel shows a regularized Sinkhorn-style closure with inflated intermediate covariances. The right panel shows a drifting-style closure with a curved mean path and moment-matched covariance ellipses.

Interactive panel. Use the anisotropy, angle, regularization, and drift controls to compare Gaussian closures of Wasserstein, Sinkhorn, and drifting dynamics.

Gaussianity preservation.¶

The first question is invariance: one wants a simple criterion ensuring that the continuity equation does not leave the finite-dimensional Gaussian family.

Proposition: Affine Velocities Preserve Gaussianity

Let αt=N(mt,Σt)\alpha_t=\Gaussian(\mean_t,\cov_t)αt​=N(mt​,Σt​), with Σt\cov_tΣt​ positive definite, solve the continuity equation with an affine velocity

vt(x)=bt+At(x−mt).v_t(x)=b_t+A_t(x-\mean_t).vt​(x)=bt​+At​(x−mt​).
(63)

Then αt\alpha_tαt​ remains Gaussian and its moments solve

m˙t=bt,Σ˙t=AtΣt+ΣtAt⊤.\dot \mean_t=b_t, \qquad \dot\cov_t=A_t\cov_t+\cov_t A_t^\top .m˙t​=bt​,Σ˙t​=At​Σt​+Σt​At⊤​.
(64)

Conversely, any smooth Gaussian curve with positive definite covariance can be generated by such an affine velocity. If one wants the velocity to be a Wasserstein tangent gradient, one chooses the unique symmetric solution of the Lyapunov equation

AtΣt+ΣtAt=Σ˙t.A_t\cov_t+\cov_t A_t=\dot\cov_t.At​Σt​+Σt​At​=Σ˙t​.
(65)
Proof

Let XtX_tXt​ follow the characteristic ODE X˙t=bt+At(Xt−mt)\dot X_t=b_t+A_t(X_t-\mean_t)X˙t​=bt​+At​(Xt​−mt​). This linear ODE maps Gaussian random variables to Gaussian random variables. Taking expectation gives m˙t=bt\dot\mean_t=b_tm˙t​=bt​. Writing X~t=Xt−mt\tilde X_t=X_t-\mean_tX~t​=Xt​−mt​, one has X~˙t=AtX~t\dot{\tilde X}_t=A_t\tilde X_tX~˙t​=At​X~t​, hence

Σ˙t= d dtE(X~tX~t⊤)=AtΣt+ΣtAt⊤.\dot\cov_t = \frac{\d}{\d t}\EE(\tilde X_t\tilde X_t^\top) = A_t\cov_t+\cov_t A_t^\top .Σ˙t​=dtd​E(X~t​X~t⊤​)=At​Σt​+Σt​At⊤​.
(66)

For the converse, set bt=m˙tb_t=\dot\mean_tbt​=m˙t​ and choose any matrix AtA_tAt​ satisfying the covariance equation. Since Σt\cov_tΣt​ is positive definite, the Lyapunov map A↦AΣt+ΣtAA\mapsto A\cov_t+\cov_t AA↦AΣt​+Σt​A is invertible on symmetric matrices, which gives the unique symmetric choice when a gradient velocity is required. In that case vtv_tvt​ is the gradient of the quadratic potential x↦⟨bt,x⟩+⟨At(x−mt),x−mt⟩/2x\mapsto \dotp{b_t}{x}+\dotp{A_t(x-\mean_t)}{x-\mean_t}/2x↦⟨bt​,x⟩+⟨At​(x−mt​),x−mt​⟩/2.

Constrained evolution on the Gaussian manifold.¶

For non-affine velocities, the finite-dimensional substitute is to project the Wasserstein dynamics onto the Gaussian manifold.

Let

G={N(m,Σ):m∈Rd, Σ≻0}\mathcal G=\{\Gaussian(\mean,\cov):\mean\in\RR^d,\ \cov\succ0\}G={N(m,Σ):m∈Rd, Σ≻0}
(67)

be the Gaussian submanifold of P2(Rd)\Pp_2(\RR^d)P2​(Rd). The Wasserstein gradient of a functional constrained to a smooth submanifold M⊂P2\mathcal M\subset\Pp_2M⊂P2​ is defined as the Riesz representative of the differential restricted to tangent velocities of M\mathcal MM. Equivalently, it is the small-step limit of the constrained JKO scheme

αk+1∈arg min⁡α∈M12τW22(α,αk)+f(α).\alpha^{k+1}\in \argmin_{\alpha\in\mathcal M} \frac{1}{2\tau}\Wass_2^2(\alpha,\alpha^k)+f(\alpha).αk+1∈α∈Margmin​2τ1​W22​(α,αk)+f(α).
(68)

For M=G\mathcal M=\mathcal GM=G, tangent velocities are affine gradient fields v(x)=b+A(x−m)v(x)=b+A(x-\mean)v(x)=b+A(x−m) with A=A⊤A=A^\topA=A⊤. The constrained gradient is therefore the L2(N(m,Σ))L^2(\Gaussian(\mean,\cov))L2(N(m,Σ)) projection of the ambient Wasserstein gradient onto this finite-dimensional affine space, whenever the ambient gradient exists.

Proposition: Gaussian-Constrained Wasserstein Gradients

Let fff be a smooth functional and assume that its restriction to nondegenerate Gaussian measures can be written as

f(N(m,Σ))=F(m,Σ).f(\Gaussian(\mean,\cov))=F(\mean,\cov).f(N(m,Σ))=F(m,Σ).
(69)

Then the Wasserstein gradient constrained to the Gaussian family is the affine vector field

vF(x)=∇mF(m,Σ)+2∇ΣF(m,Σ)(x−m),v_F(x) = \nabla_\mean F(\mean,\cov) + 2\nabla_\cov F(\mean,\cov)(x-\mean),vF​(x)=∇m​F(m,Σ)+2∇Σ​F(m,Σ)(x−m),
(70)

where ∇ΣF\nabla_\cov F∇Σ​F denotes the symmetric matrix derivative. Equivalently, vFv_FvF​ is the L2(N(m,Σ))L^2(\Gaussian(\mean,\cov))L2(N(m,Σ)) projection of the ambient Wasserstein gradient onto affine gradient fields, whenever the ambient gradient exists. Hence the gradient descent flow constrained to Gaussian measures satisfies

m˙t=−∇mF(mt,Σt),Σ˙t=−2(Σt∇ΣF(mt,Σt)+∇ΣF(mt,Σt)Σt),\dot\mean_t=-\nabla_\mean F(\mean_t,\cov_t), \qquad \dot\cov_t=-2\bigl(\cov_t\nabla_\cov F(\mean_t,\cov_t)+\nabla_\cov F(\mean_t,\cov_t)\cov_t\bigr),m˙t​=−∇m​F(mt​,Σt​),Σ˙t​=−2(Σt​∇Σ​F(mt​,Σt​)+∇Σ​F(mt​,Σt​)Σt​),
(71)

and the descent velocity is affine.

Proof

Test the functional along a Gaussian tangent vector, represented by an affine gradient field

v(x)=b+A(x−m)v(x)=b+A(x-\mean)v(x)=b+A(x−m)
(72)

with AAA symmetric. The induced first-order variations are m˙=b\dot\mean=bm˙=b and Σ˙=AΣ+ΣA\dot\cov=A\cov+\cov AΣ˙=AΣ+ΣA. Therefore

 dF(m,Σ)[b,AΣ+ΣA]=⟨∇mF,b⟩+tr ⁣(∇ΣF(AΣ+ΣA)).\d F(\mean,\cov)[b,A\cov+\cov A] = \dotp{\nabla_\mean F}{b} + \mathrm{tr}\!\left(\nabla_\cov F(A\cov+\cov A)\right).dF(m,Σ)[b,AΣ+ΣA]=⟨∇m​F,b⟩+tr(∇Σ​F(AΣ+ΣA)).
(73)

Since AAA, Σ\covΣ and ∇ΣF\nabla_\cov F∇Σ​F are symmetric, the second term equals

2 tr(∇ΣF AΣ)=∫⟨2∇ΣF(x−m),A(x−m)⟩ dN(m,Σ)(x).2\,\mathrm{tr}(\nabla_\cov F\,A\cov) = \int \dotp{2\nabla_\cov F(x-\mean)}{A(x-\mean)}\d\Gaussian(\mean,\cov)(x).2tr(∇Σ​FAΣ)=∫⟨2∇Σ​F(x−m),A(x−m)⟩dN(m,Σ)(x).
(74)

Together with the mean term, this gives

 dF(m,Σ)[m˙,Σ˙]=∫⟨vF(x),v(x)⟩ dN(m,Σ)(x)\d F(\mean,\cov)[\dot\mean,\dot\cov] = \int \dotp{v_F(x)}{v(x)}\d\Gaussian(\mean,\cov)(x)dF(m,Σ)[m˙,Σ˙]=∫⟨vF​(x),v(x)⟩dN(m,Σ)(x)
(75)

for all affine gradient fields vvv. This identifies the constrained Wasserstein gradient in the induced L2(α)L^2(\alpha)L2(α) metric, or equivalently the projection of the ambient gradient when it exists. Substituting the descent velocity −vF-v_F−vF​ in the affine-velocity moment equations gives (71).

This proposition should be read as the organizing rule for Gaussian closures: once the scalar energy has been reduced to a function of (m,Σ)(\mean,\cov)(m,Σ), its constrained Wasserstein gradient is automatically affine and the covariance follows the Bures-type ODE (71). When the first variation of fff is quadratic, this constrained gradient coincides with the full Wasserstein gradient.

Gaussian-preserving gradient flows.¶

The next examples show that many familiar energies already have affine Wasserstein gradients on Gaussian inputs, so their full flow remains inside the Gaussian family.

Example

The Gaussian-constrained gradient formula turns many standard energies into explicit affine fields:

  • Quadratic potential energy. If

    f(α)=∫(12x⊤Hx+⟨ℓ,x⟩) dα(x),H=H⊤,f(\alpha)=\int \Bigl(\frac12 x^\top Hx+\dotp{\ell}{x}\Bigr)\d\alpha(x), \qquad H=H^\top,f(α)=∫(21​x⊤Hx+⟨ℓ,x⟩)dα(x),H=H⊤,
    (76)

    then

    ∇ ⁣Wf(α)(x)=Hx+ℓ=(Hm+ℓ)+H(x−m).\Wgrad f(\alpha)(x)=Hx+\ell=(H\mean+\ell)+H(x-\mean).∇W​f(α)(x)=Hx+ℓ=(Hm+ℓ)+H(x−m).
    (77)

    This is the Gaussian form of transport under a quadratic confinement.

  • Quadratic interaction energy. If

    f(α)=14∬(x−y)⊤G(x−y) dα(x) dα(y),G=G⊤,f(\alpha)=\frac14\iint (x-y)^\top G(x-y)\d\alpha(x)\d\alpha(y), \qquad G=G^\top,f(α)=41​∬(x−y)⊤G(x−y)dα(x)dα(y),G=G⊤,
    (78)

    then F(m,Σ)=12tr(GΣ)F(\mean,\cov)=\frac12\mathrm{tr}(G\cov)F(m,Σ)=21​tr(GΣ) and

    ∇ ⁣Wf(α)(x)=G(x−m).\Wgrad f(\alpha)(x)=G(x-\mean).∇W​f(α)(x)=G(x−m).
    (79)

    The mean is unchanged and the covariance contracts or expands according to the signs of GGG.

  • Relative entropy to a Gaussian. For αˉ=N(mˉ,Σˉ)\bar\alpha=\Gaussian(\bar\mean,\bar\cov)αˉ=N(mˉ,Σˉ),

    f(α)=KL⁡(α∣αˉ)f(\alpha)=\KL(\alpha|\bar\alpha)f(α)=KL(α∣αˉ)
    (80)

    has

    ∇ ⁣Wf(α)(x)=Σˉ−1(m−mˉ)+(Σˉ−1−Σ−1)(x−m).\Wgrad f(\alpha)(x) = \bar\cov^{-1}(\mean-\bar\mean) + (\bar\cov^{-1}-\cov^{-1})(x-\mean).∇W​f(α)(x)=Σˉ−1(m−mˉ)+(Σˉ−1−Σ−1)(x−m).
    (81)

    The descent equations are the Ornstein--Uhlenbeck moment equations

    m˙t=−Σˉ−1(mt−mˉ),Σ˙t=2Id−Σˉ−1Σt−ΣtΣˉ−1.\dot\mean_t=-\bar\cov^{-1}(\mean_t-\bar\mean), \qquad \dot\cov_t=2\Id-\bar\cov^{-1}\cov_t-\cov_t\bar\cov^{-1}.m˙t​=−Σˉ−1(mt​−mˉ),Σ˙t​=2Id−Σˉ−1Σt​−Σt​Σˉ−1.
    (82)

  • Squared Wasserstein distance to a Gaussian. For

    f(α)=12W22(α,αˉ),αˉ=N(mˉ,Σˉ),f(\alpha)=\frac12\Wass_2^2(\alpha,\bar\alpha), \qquad \bar\alpha=\Gaussian(\bar\mean,\bar\cov),f(α)=21​W22​(α,αˉ),αˉ=N(mˉ,Σˉ),
    (83)

    the Gaussian Brenier map Tα→αˉT_{\alpha\to\bar\alpha}Tα→αˉ​ is affine,

    Tα→αˉ(x)=mˉ+M(x−m),M=Σ−1/2(Σ1/2ΣˉΣ1/2)1/2Σ−1/2.T_{\alpha\to\bar\alpha}(x)=\bar\mean+M(x-\mean), \qquad M=\cov^{-1/2}(\cov^{1/2}\bar\cov\cov^{1/2})^{1/2}\cov^{-1/2}.Tα→αˉ​(x)=mˉ+M(x−m),M=Σ−1/2(Σ1/2ΣˉΣ1/2)1/2Σ−1/2.
    (84)

    Hence

    ∇ ⁣Wf(α)(x)=x−Tα→αˉ(x)=(m−mˉ)+(Id−M)(x−m),\Wgrad f(\alpha)(x)=x-T_{\alpha\to\bar\alpha}(x) = (\mean-\bar\mean)+(\Id-M)(x-\mean),∇W​f(α)(x)=x−Tα→αˉ​(x)=(m−mˉ)+(Id−M)(x−m),
    (85)

    and descent moves each Gaussian infinitesimally along the Bures--Wasserstein geodesic toward αˉ\bar\alphaαˉ.

  • Gaussian-only losses. Sliced SW⁡22\SW_2^2SW22​ losses to a Gaussian, Gaussian Sinkhorn divergences, and any smooth closed formula depending only on (m,Σ)(\mean,\cov)(m,Σ) fit the same constrained-gradient template:

    vF(x)=∇mF+2∇ΣF(x−m).v_F(x)=\nabla_\mean F+2\nabla_\cov F(x-\mean).vF​(x)=∇m​F+2∇Σ​F(x−m).
    (86)

    For Gaussian Sinkhorn divergences this finite-dimensional flow is studied in Hardion & Lacombe, 2026.

Not every PDE preserves Gaussianity exactly. For instance, Wasserstein flows of relative Fisher information, related to quantum-drift or higher-order diffusion equations, typically require a Gaussian projection to close on (m,Σ)(\mean,\cov)(m,Σ). Such projected closures are still useful: they expose the finite-dimensional dynamics predicted by a variational model and make it easy to compare variational flows with non-variational affine dynamics such as drifting fields or the Gaussian transformer closure below.

Proposition: Centered Gaussian Covariance Catalogue

Let γ=N(0,Id)\gamma=\Gaussian(0,\Id)γ=N(0,Id) and let μt=N(0,Ct)\mu_t=\Gaussian(0,C_t)μt​=N(0,Ct​) with Ct≻0C_t\succ0Ct​≻0. For the normalizations displayed below, the Wasserstein descent constrained to the centered Gaussian manifold satisfies C˙t=h(Ct)\dot C_t=h(C_t)C˙t​=h(Ct​), with

KL⁡(μ∣γ):h(C)=2(Id−C),12 I(μ∣γ):h(C)=2(C−1−C),W22(μ,γ):h(C)=4(C1/2−C),MMD⁡k2(μ,γ),k(x,y)=⟨x,y⟩2:h(C)=8(C−C2),Sϵ(μ,γ):h(C)=4(C+ϵ216Id)1/2−2(C2+ϵ216Id)1/2−2C−ϵ2Id,SW⁡22(μ,γ):h(C)=V(C)C+CV(C),\begin{array}{rcl} \KL(\mu|\gamma) &:& h(C)=2(\Id-C), \\[.35em] \frac12\,\mathcal I(\mu|\gamma) &:& h(C)=2(C^{-1}-C), \\[.35em] \Wass_2^2(\mu,\gamma) &:& h(C)=4(C^{1/2}-C), \\[.35em] \MMD_k^2(\mu,\gamma),\quad k(x,y)=\dotp{x}{y}^2 &:& h(C)=8(C-C^2), \\[.35em] S_\epsilon(\mu,\gamma) &:& h(C)= 4\left(C+\frac{\epsilon^2}{16}\Id\right)^{1/2} -2\left(C^2+\frac{\epsilon^2}{16}\Id\right)^{1/2} -2C-\frac{\epsilon}{2}\Id, \\[.75em] \SW_2^2(\mu,\gamma) &:& h(C)=V(C)C+CV(C), \end{array}KL(μ∣γ)21​I(μ∣γ)W22​(μ,γ)MMDk2​(μ,γ),k(x,y)=⟨x,y⟩2Sϵ​(μ,γ)SW22​(μ,γ)​::::::​h(C)=2(Id−C),h(C)=2(C−1−C),h(C)=4(C1/2−C),h(C)=8(C−C2),h(C)=4(C+16ϵ2​Id)1/2−2(C2+16ϵ2​Id)1/2−2C−2ϵ​Id,h(C)=V(C)C+CV(C),​
(87)

where SϵS_\epsilonSϵ​ is the debiased Sinkhorn divergence for the quadratic cost ∥x−y∥2\norm{x-y}^2∥x−y∥2 and KL regularization strength ϵ\epsilonϵ, and

V(C)=2∫Sd−1(1θ⊤Cθ−1)θθ⊤  dσ(θ)V(C)= 2\int_{\Sphere^{d-1}} \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\theta^\top C\theta}}-1\right) \theta\theta^\top \,\d\sigma(\theta)V(C)=2∫Sd−1​(θ⊤Cθ​1​−1)θθ⊤dσ(θ)
(88)

for the normalized spherical measure σ\sigmaσ. Here

I(μ∣γ)=∫∣∇log⁡ρ(x)+x∣2ρ(x)  dx(μ=ρ  dx).\mathcal I(\mu|\gamma)=\int \left|\nabla\log\rho(x)+x\right|^2\rho(x)\,\d x \qquad(\mu=\rho\,\d x).I(μ∣γ)=∫∣∇logρ(x)+x∣2ρ(x)dx(μ=ρdx).
(89)

Thus the unhalved Fisher divergence has right-hand side 4(C−1−C)4(C^{-1}-C)4(C−1−C). Multiplying any of these energies by a constant simply rescales the corresponding right-hand side.

Proof

Each row is obtained by identifying the affine descent velocity v(x)=MCxv(x)=M_Cxv(x)=MC​x generated by the corresponding Gaussian-constrained calculation and then applying the affine covariance equation, which gives C˙=MCC+CMC⊤\dot C=M_CC+CM_C^\topC˙=MC​C+CMC⊤​. For KL⁡(⋅∣γ)\KL(\cdot|\gamma)KL(⋅∣γ), the Fokker--Planck velocity is (C−1−Id)x(C^{-1}-\Id)x(C−1−Id)x, hence C˙=2(Id−C)\dot C=2(\Id-C)C˙=2(Id−C). For the Fisher row, the restriction of 12I\frac12\mathcal I21​I to centered Gaussians is

12(tr⁡(C)+tr⁡(C−1)−2d).\frac12\left(\tr(C)+\tr(C^{-1})-2d\right).21​(tr(C)+tr(C−1)−2d).
(90)

Using the Gaussian-constrained gradient formula gives the descent velocity (C−2−Id)x(C^{-2}-\Id)x(C−2−Id)x, hence C˙=2(C−1−C)\dot C=2(C^{-1}-C)C˙=2(C−1−C). This row should be read as a Gaussian projected closure of the fourth-order Fisher flow.

For W22(⋅,γ)\Wass_2^2(\cdot,\gamma)W22​(⋅,γ), the Brenier map from N(0,C)\Gaussian(0,C)N(0,C) to γ\gammaγ is C−1/2xC^{-1/2}xC−1/2x, so the descent velocity for the unhalved squared distance is 2(C−1/2−Id)x2(C^{-1/2}-\Id)x2(C−1/2−Id)x, giving 4(C1/2−C)4(C^{1/2}-C)4(C1/2−C). For the polynomial MMD row, centered Gaussians satisfy MMD⁡k2(μ,γ)=∥C−Id∥F2\MMD_k^2(\mu,\gamma)=\norm{C-\Id}_{\mathrm{F}}^2MMDk2​(μ,γ)=∥C−Id∥F2​; the first variation is quadratic and its descent velocity is 4(Id−C)x4(\Id-C)x4(Id−C)x, giving 8(C−C2)8(C-C^2)8(C−C2).

Gaussian Sinkhorn dual potentials are quadratic, so the velocity is again linear; differentiating the closed Gaussian formula yields the displayed spectral expression. The square roots are spectral functions of CCC, hence commute with CCC, which is why the covariance ODE closes as a matrix function of CCC alone. For sliced Wasserstein, each one-dimensional projection is a Gaussian transport with velocity 2((θ⊤Cθ)−1/2−1)⟨θ,x⟩θ2((\theta^\top C\theta)^{-1/2}-1)\dotp{\theta}{x}\theta2((θ⊤Cθ)−1/2−1)⟨θ,x⟩θ; averaging these velocities over Sd−1\Sphere^{d-1}Sd−1 gives v(x)=V(C)xv(x)=V(C)xv(x)=V(C)x and thus C˙=V(C)C+CV(C)\dot C=V(C)C+CV(C)C˙=V(C)C+CV(C).

Example

In the two-layer model above, take the linear activation σ(s)=s\sigma(s)=sσ(s)=s, so that

ψ((u,v),z)=v ⟨u,z⟩.\psi((u,v),z)=v\,\dotp{u}{z}.ψ((u,v),z)=v⟨u,z⟩.
(91)

The predictor is the linear map

Gαt(z)=Qtz,Qt=∫vu⊤ dαt(u,v)∈Rd′×d.G_{\alpha_t}(z)=Q_t z, \qquad Q_t=\int v u^\top\d\alpha_t(u,v)\in\RR^{d'\times d}.Gαt​​(z)=Qt​z,Qt​=∫vu⊤dαt​(u,v)∈Rd′×d.
(92)

Thus the energy depends on the neuron law only through the cross moment QtQ_tQt​, a subblock of the raw second moment of x=(u,v)x=(u,v)x=(u,v). For square loss, set

S=∫zz⊤ dρ(z,y),R=∫yz⊤ dρ(z,y),Ht=QtS−R.S=\int zz^\top\d\rho(z,y), \qquad R=\int y z^\top\d\rho(z,y), \qquad H_t=Q_tS-R.S=∫zz⊤dρ(z,y),R=∫yz⊤dρ(z,y),Ht​=Qt​S−R.
(93)

The first variation is

δf(αt)(u,v)=⟨Ht,vu⊤⟩=v⊤Htu.\delta f(\alpha_t)(u,v)=\dotp{H_t}{v u^\top}=v^\top H_t u.δf(αt​)(u,v)=⟨Ht​,vu⊤⟩=v⊤Ht​u.
(94)

Hence the particle velocity in parameter space is linear:

−∇(u,v)δf(αt)(u,v)=−(0Ht⊤Ht0)(uv).-\nabla_{(u,v)}\delta f(\alpha_t)(u,v) = -\begin{pmatrix} 0 & H_t^\top \\ H_t & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \end{pmatrix}.−∇(u,v)​δf(αt​)(u,v)=−(0Ht​​Ht⊤​0​)(uv​).
(95)

Therefore a Gaussian law of neurons remains Gaussian. Its mean and covariance follow the affine-velocity moment equations, with a matrix depending only on the current cross moment QtQ_tQt​, a raw second-moment subblock that becomes a covariance subblock when the neuron law is centered. This exact closure is special to the linear activation; for nonlinear activations, Gaussian closures are usually projections rather than invariant families.

Non-variational Gaussian-preserving flows.¶

The last examples are not ordinary gradient flows of a fixed scalar energy on the full Wasserstein space. They preserve Gaussianity because the prescribed velocity field is affine when evaluated on Gaussian measures.

Example

Consider a prescribed Gaussian interpolation αt=N(mt,Σt)\alpha_t=\Gaussian(\mean_t,\cov_t)αt​=N(mt​,Σt​). The affine-velocity criterion shows that an exact flow-matching velocity can be taken affine:

vt(x)=m˙t+At(x−mt),AtΣt+ΣtAt=Σ˙t.v_t(x)=\dot\mean_t+A_t(x-\mean_t), \qquad A_t\cov_t+\cov_t A_t=\dot\cov_t.vt​(x)=m˙t​+At​(x−mt​),At​Σt​+Σt​At​=Σ˙t​.
(96)

In the isotropic case Σt=st2Id\cov_t=s_t^2\IdΣt​=st2​Id, this reduces to the transparent formula

vt(x)=m˙t+s˙tst(x−mt).v_t(x)=\dot\mean_t+\frac{\dot s_t}{s_t}(x-\mean_t).vt​(x)=m˙t​+st​s˙t​​(x−mt​).
(97)

For instance, the diffusion noising path

Xt=atX0+σtZ,Z∼N(0,Id),X_t=a_tX_0+\sigma_t Z,\qquad Z\sim\Gaussian(0,\Id),Xt​=at​X0​+σt​Z,Z∼N(0,Id),
(98)

has mt=atm0\mean_t=a_t\mean_0mt​=at​m0​ and Σt=at2Σ0+σt2Id\cov_t=a_t^2\cov_0+\sigma_t^2\IdΣt​=at2​Σ0​+σt2​Id. Thus, in the Gaussian case, diffusion paths and flow-matching paths reduce to the same mean-covariance bookkeeping, although the corresponding training objectives are different.

Example

Let the target be β=N(mˉ,Σˉ)\beta=\Gaussian(\bar\mean,\bar\cov)β=N(mˉ,Σˉ) and assume μt=N(mt,Σt)\mu_t=\Gaussian(\mean_t,\cov_t)μt​=N(mt​,Σt​). For the Gaussian kernel

Kϵ(x,y)=exp⁡(−∥x−y∥2/(2ϵ)),K_\epsilon(x,y)=\exp(-\norm{x-y}^2/(2\epsilon)),Kϵ​(x,y)=exp(−∥x−y∥2/(2ϵ)),
(99)

the normalized field (46) satisfies

Bϵ[μt](x)=−ϵ(Σt+ϵId)−1(x−mt).B_\epsilon[\mu_t](x) = -\epsilon(\cov_t+\epsilon\Id)^{-1}(x-\mean_t).Bϵ​[μt​](x)=−ϵ(Σt​+ϵId)−1(x−mt​).
(100)

Thus the drifting velocity (48) is affine and preserves Gaussianity. With

At=(Σt+ϵId)−1,Aˉ=(Σˉ+ϵId)−1,A_t=(\cov_t+\epsilon\Id)^{-1}, \qquad \bar A=(\bar\cov+\epsilon\Id)^{-1},At​=(Σt​+ϵId)−1,Aˉ=(Σˉ+ϵId)−1,
(101)

the ODE is

m˙t=ϵAˉ(mˉ−mt),Σ˙t=ϵ((At−Aˉ)Σt+Σt(At−Aˉ)).\dot\mean_t=\epsilon\bar A(\bar\mean-\mean_t), \qquad \dot\cov_t=\epsilon\bigl((A_t-\bar A)\cov_t+\cov_t(A_t-\bar A)\bigr).m˙t​=ϵAˉ(mˉ−mt​),Σ˙t​=ϵ((At​−Aˉ)Σt​+Σt​(At​−Aˉ)).
(102)

This finite-dimensional model explains the stabilizing role of the self-normalized repulsion term in drifting: without it, the covariance equation loses the AtΣt+ΣtAtA_t\cov_t+\cov_tA_tAt​Σt​+Σt​At​ contribution.

Example

For the transformer PDE, assume α=N(m,Σ)\alpha=\Gaussian(\mean,\cov)α=N(m,Σ). Since exponential tilting preserves Gaussianity,

∫e⟨Qx,Ky⟩ y  dα(y)∫e⟨Qx,Kz⟩  dα(z)=m+ΣK⊤Qx.\frac{\int e^{\dotp{Qx}{Ky}}\,y\,\d\alpha(y)} {\int e^{\dotp{Qx}{Kz}}\,\d\alpha(z)} = \mean+\cov K^\top Qx.∫e⟨Qx,Kz⟩dα(z)∫e⟨Qx,Ky⟩ydα(y)​=m+ΣK⊤Qx.
(103)

Therefore

Γθ[α](x)=Vm+VΣK⊤Qx\Gamma_\theta[\alpha](x)=V\mean+V\cov K^\top QxΓθ​[α](x)=Vm+VΣK⊤Qx
(104)

is affine. The Gaussian token law is preserved and satisfies

m˙t=(Vt+VtΣtKt⊤Qt)mt,Σ˙t=BtΣt+ΣtBt⊤,Bt=VtΣtKt⊤Qt.\dot\mean_t=(V_t+V_t\cov_tK_t^\top Q_t)\mean_t, \qquad \dot\cov_t=B_t\cov_t+\cov_tB_t^\top, \qquad B_t=V_t\cov_tK_t^\top Q_t.m˙t​=(Vt​+Vt​Σt​Kt⊤​Qt​)mt​,Σ˙t​=Bt​Σt​+Σt​Bt⊤​,Bt​=Vt​Σt​Kt⊤​Qt​.
(105)

When Vt=Qt⊤KtV_t=Q_t^\top K_tVt​=Qt⊤​Kt​, the matrix Bt=Qt⊤KtΣtKt⊤QtB_t=Q_t^\top K_t\cov_tK_t^\top Q_tBt​=Qt⊤​Kt​Σt​Kt⊤​Qt​ is symmetric positive semidefinite, matching the gradient-field case mentioned above. This closure is not a convergence theorem for trained transformers. It is instead a tractable model of how attention can shear, amplify or contract a cloud of tokens through its covariance.

Contractive Gaussian projection.¶

The preceding examples show when Gaussianity is preserved or imposed by projection. Gelbrich’s inequality Gelbrich, 1990 gives a useful variational explanation: replacing a measure by the Gaussian with the same first two moments cannot increase its Wasserstein distance to another similarly projected measure.

Theorem: Gelbrich Projection

For μ∈P2(Rd)\mu\in\Pp_2(\RR^d)μ∈P2​(Rd), let

Rμ:=N(mμ,Σμ)\mathcal R\mu\eqdef \Gaussian(\mean_\mu,\cov_\mu)Rμ:=N(mμ​,Σμ​)
(106)

be the Gaussian with the same mean and covariance as μ\muμ. Then

W22(Rμ,Rν)=∥mμ−mν∥2+B2(Σμ,Σν)≤W22(μ,ν).\Wass_2^2(\mathcal R\mu,\mathcal R\nu) = \norm{\mean_\mu-\mean_\nu}^2+\Bb^2(\cov_\mu,\cov_\nu) \leq \Wass_2^2(\mu,\nu).W22​(Rμ,Rν)=∥mμ​−mν​∥2+B2(Σμ​,Σν​)≤W22​(μ,ν).
(107)
Proof

Take any coupling (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y) of μ\muμ and ν\nuν, center the variables, and write C=E[(X−mμ)(Y−mν)⊤]C=\EE[(X-\mean_\mu)(Y-\mean_\nu)^\top]C=E[(X−mμ​)(Y−mν​)⊤]. In the positive definite case, positivity of the block covariance matrix implies the factorization C=Σμ1/2KΣν1/2C=\cov_\mu^{1/2}K\cov_\nu^{1/2}C=Σμ1/2​KΣν1/2​ with ∥K∥op≤1\norm{K}_{\mathrm{op}}\leq1∥K∥op​≤1, and therefore, by operator/nuclear norm duality,

tr⁡C≤tr⁡((Σμ1/2ΣνΣμ1/2)1/2).\tr C\leq \tr\left((\cov_\mu^{1/2}\cov_\nu\cov_\mu^{1/2})^{1/2}\right).trC≤tr((Σμ1/2​Σν​Σμ1/2​)1/2).
(108)

The semidefinite case follows by adding ηId\eta\IdηId to both covariance matrices and letting η↓0\eta\downarrow0η↓0. Expanding E∥X−Y∥2\EE\norm{X-Y}^2E∥X−Y∥2 gives the lower bound

E∥X−Y∥2≥∥mμ−mν∥2+B2(Σμ,Σν).\EE\norm{X-Y}^2 \geq \norm{\mean_\mu-\mean_\nu}^2+\Bb^2(\cov_\mu,\cov_\nu).E∥X−Y∥2≥∥mμ​−mν​∥2+B2(Σμ​,Σν​).
(109)

Taking the infimum over couplings proves the inequality, while equality for Gaussian laws is exactly the Gaussian Bures formula.

The following preservation criterion is a direct consequence of Gelbrich’s theorem and was explained to us by Hugo Lavenant. It says that a functional which does not increase under moment-matched Gaussian projection admits Gaussian minimizing movements from Gaussian initial data.

Theorem: Hugo Lavenant Gaussian-Preservation Criterion

Let F:P2(Rd)→(−∞,+∞]F:\Pp_2(\RR^d)\to(-\infty,+\infty]F:P2​(Rd)→(−∞,+∞] satisfy

F(Rμ)≤F(μ)∀μ∈P2(Rd),F(\mathcal R\mu)\leq F(\mu) \qquad\forall\mu\in\Pp_2(\RR^d),F(Rμ)≤F(μ)∀μ∈P2​(Rd),
(110)

with R\mathcal RR defined in Gelbrich’s theorem above. If γ\gammaγ is Gaussian and ν\nuν minimizes the JKO step

η↦F(η)+12τW22(γ,η),\eta\mapsto F(\eta)+\frac1{2\tau}\Wass_2^2(\gamma,\eta),η↦F(η)+2τ1​W22​(γ,η),
(111)

then Rν\mathcal R\nuRν is also a minimizer. If the JKO minimizer is unique, it is Gaussian. Thus any unique Wasserstein gradient flow obtained as the limit of this JKO scheme preserves Gaussian initial data.

Proof

For the JKO claim, Rγ=γ\mathcal R\gamma=\gammaRγ=γ because γ\gammaγ is Gaussian. Hence, for any competitor η\etaη,

F(Rη)+12τW22(γ,Rη)≤F(η)+12τW22(γ,η).F(\mathcal R\eta)+\frac1{2\tau}\Wass_2^2(\gamma,\mathcal R\eta) \leq F(\eta)+\frac1{2\tau}\Wass_2^2(\gamma,\eta).F(Rη)+2τ1​W22​(γ,Rη)≤F(η)+2τ1​W22​(γ,η).
(112)

Applying this to a minimizer η=ν\eta=\nuη=ν shows that Rν\mathcal R\nuRν is again a minimizer. Uniqueness forces ν=Rν\nu=\mathcal R\nuν=Rν.

Remark

The same projection argument also explains why quadratic Wasserstein barycenters of Gaussian measures are Gaussian. If βs\be_sβs​ are Gaussian and

F(μ)=∑sλsW22(μ,βs),F(\mu)=\sum_s\la_s\Wass_2^2(\mu,\be_s),F(μ)=s∑​λs​W22​(μ,βs​),
(113)

then Rβs=βs\mathcal R\be_s=\be_sRβs​=βs​, and Gelbrich’s theorem gives F(Rμ)≤F(μ)F(\mathcal R\mu)\leq F(\mu)F(Rμ)≤F(μ). Thus the moment-matched Gaussian projection of any barycenter is again a barycenter; when the barycenter is unique, it must itself be Gaussian. This is the contraction viewpoint behind the Gaussian barycenter corollary.

References¶
  1. Peyré, G. (2025). Optimal and Diffusion Transports in Machine Learning. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2512.06797.
  2. Hyvärinen, A. (2005). Estimation of Non-Normalized Statistical Models by Score Matching. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 6, 695–709.
  3. Vincent, P. (2011). A Connection Between Score Matching and Denoising Autoencoders. Neural Computation, 23(7), 1661–1674.
  4. Sohl-Dickstein, J., Weiss, E. A., Maheswaranathan, N., & Ganguli, S. (2015). Deep Unsupervised Learning Using Nonequilibrium Thermodynamics. Proceedings of the 32nd International Conference on Machine Learning, 37, 2256–2265.
  5. Ho, J., Jain, A., & Abbeel, P. (2020). Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 6840–6851.
  6. Song, Y., & Ermon, S. (2019). Generative Modeling by Estimating Gradients of the Data Distribution. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 32.
  7. Song, Y., Sohl-Dickstein, J., Kingma, D. P., Kumar, A., Ermon, S., & Poole, B. (2021). Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations. International Conference on Learning Representations.
  8. Lipman, Y., Chen, R. T. Q., Ben-Hamu, H., Nickel, M., & Le, M. (2023). Flow Matching for Generative Modeling. International Conference on Learning Representations.
  9. Liu, X., Gong, C., & Liu, Q. (2023). Flow Straight and Fast: Learning to Generate and Transfer Data with Rectified Flow. International Conference on Learning Representations.
  10. Albergo, M. S., Boffi, N. M., & Vanden-Eijnden, E. (2025). Stochastic Interpolants: A Unifying Framework for Flows and Diffusions. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 26(209), 1–80.
  11. Hertrich, J., Chambolle, A., & Delon, J. (2025). On the Relation between Rectified Flows and Optimal Transport. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems.
  12. Lavenant, H., & Santambrogio, F. (2022). The Flow Map of the Fokker–Planck Equation Does Not Provide Optimal Transport. Applied Mathematics Letters, 133, 108225.
  13. Han, J., Li, P., Guo, Q., Xu, R., Ermon, S., & Candès, E. J. (2026). One-Step Generative Modeling via Wasserstein Gradient Flows. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2605.11755.
  14. Deng, M., Li, H., Li, T., Du, Y., & He, K. (2026). Generative Modeling via Drifting. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2602.04770.
  15. Gretton, A., Li, K. W., Galashov, A., Thornton, J., De Bortoli, V., & Doucet, A. (2026). On the Wasserstein Gradient Flow Interpretation of Drifting Models. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2605.05118.
